title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Type 1 Credit Prices Continue To Rise; Trade Volumes Decline WoW
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights: (11th â€“ 17th August 2025)
  • A total of 143,819 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 35.19% fall week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a 59.57% fall WoW as 335,782 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $57.10/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 0.37% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers declined by 4.89% in price, reaching $56.35/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price declined by 0.79% WoW; on 19th August, the spot price stood at $56.50/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $58.38/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices rose by 0.46% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 prices fell by 3.54%, 3.57% and 3.54% WoW respectively.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

In the past week, Type 1 trade prices increased by 0.37% while trade volumes declined. Clean Fuel Alliance America has appreciated the Environmental Protection Agencyâ€™s Proposed Renewable Fuel Standards for the year 2016 and 2027. The goal of EPA to consistently work towards RFS growth in recognition of the interests of the US farmers and providers of feedstock, and investments in biomass-based diesel producers. Proposals for a robust step-change in biomass-based diesel and advanced biofuel volumes have been made by the EPA, starting in 2026. The EPA aims to reach 5.61 billion gallons in 2026 â€“ more than two billion gallons higher than in 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_602406" align="alignnone" width="901"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.37% WoW rise, increasing from $56.89 to $57.10 this week, and all non-zero credit prices decreased by 4.89% WoW, from$59.25to $56.35. Type 1 trades witnessed 143,819 credits exchanged, reflecting a 35.19% fall WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw a 59.57% fall WoW, reaching 335,782 credits. [caption id="attachment_602408" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_602407" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices fell by 0.79% WoW, reaching $57.55/MT. On 19th August, the spot price was $57.0/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $58.38MT as of 19th August.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_602417" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 48 trades were reported in the market, with no high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $74.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 5,000 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $46.00 per credit of one Type 2 transaction reported for 12,000 credits. The largest volume of transfers involved 20,000 credits for Type 2 as well Type 1 transactions at $57.75 and $60.00 respectively, while the smallest volume of transfer involved 283 credits for a Type 1 transaction priced at $51.24.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_602410" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $56.68 in August so far, up from $47.99 in July. Trade volumes for the month of August stood at 1.29 M as of 17th August, 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_602413" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 11th â€“ 17th August, total VPT recorded a fall of 2.11% WoW, with a decline in all non-zero credit prices for the last week. The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls.

Â Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose by 0.46% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 prices fell by 3.54%, 3.57% and 3.54% WoW, respectively. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming Dates

Â Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this Week

  • Clean Fuels Alliance America Asks EPA to Sustain Robust Biomass-based Diesel Volumes in 2026-27 RFS: Clean Fuels Alliance America filed comments on the Environmental Protection Agencyâ€™s Proposed Renewable Fuel Standards for 2026 and 2027. Clean Fuels expressed appreciation for EPAâ€™s intent to provide consistent RFS growth in recognition of U.S. farmersâ€™, feedstock providersâ€™, and biomass-based diesel producersâ€™ investments in new capacity. EPA proposes a robust step-change in biomass-based diesel and advanced biofuel volumes beginning in 2026. The agency projects the requirement to reach 5.61 billion gallons in 2026 â€“ more than two billion gallons higher than in 2025. According to an EPA fact sheet, â€œThese volume requirements, if finalized, will protect investments made by American corn and soybean growers, oilseed processors, and biodiesel and renewable diesel producers, whose products are critical to our countryâ€™s energy security and keeping rural economies strong.â€
  • SANDAG Launches New Fully Electric Transportation Service in Southeastern San Diego: SANDAG Transportation Committee Vice Chair and San Diego City Councilmember Sean Elo-Rivera joined partners from the Urban Collaborative Project at Market Creek Plaza to officially launch Southeastern Connect, a four-year pilot program that will offer a new, fully electric, on-demand micro transit service in the Southeastern neighbourhoods of the City of San Diego, including Chollas View, Emerald Hills, and Mount Hope. The new service, operated by Via Transportation, will provide zero-emission rides for up to four passengers within the Southeastern community.
  • President Trumpâ€™s Transportation Secretary Sean P. Duffy Unveils Revised NEVI Guidance to Allow States to Actually Build EV Chargers:S. Transportation Secretary Sean P. Duffy unveiled revised guidance for the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) Formula Program. The updates streamline applications, provide states with more flexibility, and slash red tape left by the Biden-Buttigieg Administration. The Interim Final Guidance is effective immediately while FHWA seeks comment on what further changes may be appropriate.

author: Radhika Khandelwal
